title: The cellist and the commuter
summary: On a Seoul subway platform, a daily commuter finally asks the musician a question she has wanted to ask for a year.

body.speaker: Mei
body.text: Excuse me. I see you here every morning. I want to ask you something.

body.speaker: Mei
body.text: Most people walk past you. They do not stop. They do not put money in your case. Why do you keep playing?

body.speaker: Matteo
body.text: Why do you walk past me?

body.speaker: Mei
body.text: I am late for work. Every morning. I am sorry.

body.speaker: Matteo
body.text: Do not say sorry. It is the morning. People are tired. People are late. I play for the moment, not for the money.

body.speaker: Mei
body.text: But you are a real musician. I can hear it. You should play in a concert hall.

body.speaker: Matteo
body.text: I played in concert halls for ten years. The people there listened politely. Then they went home and they forgot.

body.speaker: Matteo
body.text: Here, two people every day stop. Two people in two thousand. One of them once cried. One of them once gave me a flower. That is enough.

body.speaker: Mei
body.text: Two in two thousand sounds like very few.

body.speaker: Matteo
body.text: It is a high number. In a concert hall, three hundred people clap. But none of them cry. Not really.

body.speaker: Mei
body.text: I want to give you something. I do not have a flower. I have an apple.

body.speaker: Matteo
body.text: An apple is excellent. You are the third today. Now I will eat lunch.

annotations.meaningVi: mỗi buổi sáng (cụm chỉ tần suất)
đi ngang qua (phrasal verb: walk past)
hộp đựng (ở đây: hộp đàn để mở nhận tiền)
nhạc công / nhạc sĩ biểu diễn
khán phòng hòa nhạc / nhà hát

grammarNotes.title: 'Why' questions and giving reasons
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Để hỏi lý do dùng 'Why + động từ + chủ ngữ + …?' Ví dụ: 'Why do you keep playing?' 'Why do you walk past me?' Để trả lời có thể bắt đầu bằng lý do trực tiếp (không cần 'Because'): 'I am late for work.' Hoặc dùng 'Because + mệnh đề': 'Because I am late for work.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Ask reasons with 'Why + auxiliary + subject + verb?' From the dialogue: 'Why do you keep playing?' 'Why do you walk past me?' You can answer with the reason directly (no 'because' needed): 'I am late for work.' — or with 'Because + clause': 'Because I am late for work.'

translationVi:
Mei: Xin lỗi anh. Em thấy anh ở đây mỗi sáng. Em muốn hỏi anh một câu.
Matteo: Tất nhiên rồi. Em cứ hỏi.
Mei: Phần lớn mọi người đi ngang qua anh. Không ai dừng lại. Không ai bỏ tiền vào hộp đàn. Sao anh vẫn chơi?
Matteo: Sao em đi ngang qua tôi?
Mei: Em đi làm muộn. Mỗi sáng đều thế. Em xin lỗi.
Matteo: Đừng xin lỗi. Là buổi sáng. Mọi người mệt. Mọi người trễ. Tôi chơi cho khoảnh khắc, không cho tiền.
Mei: Nhưng anh là nhạc công thực thụ. Em nghe được. Anh nên chơi trong nhà hát.
Matteo: Tôi từng chơi trong nhà hát mười năm. Khán giả ở đó nghe lịch sự. Rồi họ về nhà và quên.
Mei: Còn ở đây?
Matteo: Ở đây, mỗi ngày có hai người dừng lại. Hai trên hai nghìn. Một người từng khóc. Một người từng cho tôi một bông hoa. Vậy là đủ.
Mei: Hai trên hai nghìn nghe ít quá.
Matteo: Đó là con số cao. Trong nhà hát ba trăm người vỗ tay. Nhưng không ai khóc. Không thật sự khóc.
Mei: Em muốn tặng anh một thứ. Em không có hoa. Em có quả táo.
Matteo: Quả táo tuyệt vời. Em là người thứ ba hôm nay. Bây giờ tôi sẽ ăn trưa.

questions.prompt: Where does the conversation take place?
questions.explanation: Implied by the title and the morning commuter context.
questions.hint: Look at the title and setting cues.

questions.prompt: Why has Mei walked past Matteo every morning?
questions.explanation: 'I am late for work. Every morning.'

questions.prompt: Why does Matteo play despite few people stopping?
options: He plays for the moment, not for the money
He must — it is his contract
questions.explanation: 'I play for the moment, not for the money.'

questions.prompt: How long did Matteo play in concert halls?
questions.explanation: 'I played in concert halls for ten years.'

questions.prompt: What did concert hall audiences do, according to Matteo?
options: Listened politely, then went home and forgot
questions.explanation: 'The people there listened politely. Then they went home and they forgot.'

questions.prompt: What do two people every day do on the platform?
options: Stop to listen — one once cried, one once gave a flower
Sit beside him for an hour

questions.prompt: What does Matteo say about clapping vs crying?
options: Three hundred clap in a hall but none truly cry; here, someone does
He has never seen anyone cry
questions.explanation: 'In a concert hall, three hundred people clap. But none of them cry. Not really.'

questions.prompt: What does Matteo's reply 'You are the third today' suggest?
options: Three people have stopped today — above his usual count
questions.explanation: He counts the rare stoppers — Mei is one.
questions.hint: Inference from earlier 'two in two thousand'.

options: A small amount of real attention can mean more than a large amount of polite attention
questions.explanation: The whole dialogue contrasts polite clapping with one real stop.

cloze.template:
Mei: Excuse me. I see you here every {{b1}}. I want to ask you something.
Matteo: Of course. Please.
Mei: Most people walk past you. They do not stop. They do not put money in your {{b2}}. Why do you keep playing?
Matteo: Why do you walk past me?
Mei: I am {{b3}} for work. Every morning. I am sorry.
Matteo: Do not say sorry. It is the morning. People are tired. People are late. I play for the {{b4}}, not for the money.
Mei: But you are a real musician. I can hear it. You should play in a concert {{b5}}.
Matteo: I played in concert halls for ten years. The people there listened {{b6}}. Then they went home and they forgot.
Mei: And here?
Matteo: Here, two people every day stop. Two people in two {{b7}}. One of them once cried. One of them once gave me a flower. That is {{b8}}.
Mei: Two in two thousand sounds like very few.
Matteo: It is a high number. In a concert hall, three hundred people {{b9}}. But none of them cry. Not really.
Mei: I want to give you something. I do not have a flower. I have an apple.
Matteo: An apple is excellent. You are the third today. Now I will eat {{b10}}.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Matteo says 'two in two thousand' is a high number. Think of one piece of work you do that few people notice. Is two-in-two-thousand a low score, or a high one, to you? What would make it feel like one or the other?
